PNe serve as the falsification boundary (both ionizing and excitation electrons are collisional). For κ≈3-5, Spitzer-Härm formula overestimates parallel heat flux by 3-25×.
AI.conclusion: Diagnostic agreement in weakly collisional plasmas can be misleading; every diagnostic campaign should include at least one Type B (bulk-sampling) measurement. Heat flux models relying on spectroscopic T_e may be significantly overestimated, relevant to ITER divertor predictions.

title: Conditioning and backward errors for nonlinear eigenvalue problems with eigenvector nonlinearities
summary: We consider eigenvalue condition numbers and backward errors for a class of symmetric nonlinear eigenvalue problems with eigenvector nonlinearities. For both of these quantities, we derive explicit and computable expressions that can be evaluated with little computational effort for a given eigenpair, assuming the matrix perturbations are measured by the spectral or Frobenius norm. We also show how symmetric perturbations can be exploited in the analysis. By means of two numerical experiments we demonstrate that problems incorporating eigenvector nonlinearities potentially need to be treated with additional care, when compared to the linear or eigenvalue-nonlinear theory.

AI.tldr: This paper derives computable expressions for eigenvalue condition numbers and backward errors in symmetric nonlinear eigenvalue problems with eigenvector nonlinearities, showing these problems require more careful treatment than linear or eigenvalue-nonlinear cases.
AI.motivation: To provide tools for analyzing sensitivity and stability of symmetric nonlinear eigenvalue problems that involve eigenvector nonlinearities, which lack clear perturbation theory compared to standard eigenvalue problems.
AI.method: Derives explicit formulas for condition numbers and backward errors under spectral/Frobenius norm perturbations, considering symmetric perturbations, and validates with numerical experiments.
AI.result: Computable expressions for eigenvalue condition numbers and backward errors; demonstration that eigenvector nonlinearities introduce additional sensitivity beyond linear or eigenvalue-nonlinear cases.
AI.conclusion: Eigenvector nonlinearities require special care in perturbation analysis, and the derived formulas provide practical tools for assessing numerical reliability.

title: Threshold dynamics for the 4$d$ mass-energy double critical NLS
summary: We consider the 4$d$ mass-energy double critical NLS \[ (i\partial_t+Δ)u = -|u|^2 u + |u| u. \] In Luo (2024) and Cheng--Miao--Zhao (2016), the authors established a scattering/blowup dichotomy for solutions satisfying the energy constraint $E(u_0)< E^c(W)$, where $W$ is the energy-critical NLS ground state and $E^c$ is the energy for the underlying cubic NLS. We prove that the scattering/blowup dichotomy persists even at the energy threshold $E(u_0)=E^c(W)$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that the scattering/blowup dichotomy for the 4D mass-energy double critical NLS holds even at the energy threshold E(u0)=E^c(W), extending previous results that required the energy to be strictly below this threshold.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the scattering/blowup dichotomy for the 4D mass-energy double critical NLS remains valid at the critical energy threshold, not just below it.
AI.method: The authors analyze the threshold case E(u0)=E^c(W) for the double critical NLS equation, building on prior work by Luo (2024) and Cheng-Miao-Zhao (2016) that established the dichotomy for subthreshold energies.
AI.result: The scattering/blowup dichotomy persists at the exact energy threshold E(u0)=E^c(W).
AI.conclusion: The threshold energy case does not break the dichotomy; solutions at this critical energy level still exhibit either scattering or blowup behavior.

title: Hybrid Machine Learning Model for Forest Height Estimation from TanDEM-X and Landsat Data
summary: Integrating machine learning (ML) with physical models (PM) has emerged as a promising way of retrieving geophysical parameters from remote sensing data. In this context, a ML model for estimating forest height from TanDEM-X interferometric coherence measurements has recently been proposed, that constrains the learning process through a PM. While the features used for training and inversion where selected to ensure the physical consistency of the solutions, they could not resolve all height / structure and baseline / terrain slope ambiguities in the data. To improve this, the extension of the feature space with optical Landsat data is proposed able to provide complementary information on forest type or structure. The extended model is applied and validated on several TanDEM-X acquisitions over the Gabonese Lopé national park site and assessed against airborne LiDAR measurements. Results show a 13.5% reduction in RMSE and a 16.6% reduction in MAE compared to the original hybrid model, confirming the added value of multispectral inputs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes extending a hybrid machine learning-physical model for forest height estimation from TanDEM-X data by adding optical Landsat data, achieving 13.5% RMSE and 16.6% MAE reductions over the original model in Gabon.
AI.motivation: To resolve height/structure and baseline/terrain slope ambiguities in forest height estimation from TanDEM-X coherence data that existing hybrid ML-PM models cannot fully address with SAR features alone.
AI.method: Extending a hybrid ML-PM model's feature space by incorporating optical Landsat data (providing complementary forest type/structure information) alongside TanDEM-X interferometric coherence measurements.
AI.result: 13.5% reduction in RMSE and 16.6% reduction in MAE compared to the original hybrid model, validated against airborne LiDAR in Gabon's Lopé National Park.
AI.conclusion: Multispectral inputs (Landsat) add complementary information that improves forest height retrieval accuracy in hybrid ML-PM frameworks.

title: Rectangular Multispectral Perturbation Theory
summary: We provide a first systematic treatment of so-called rectangular multispectral perturbation theory. With their paper from 2003, Hochstenbach and Plestenjak ["Backward Error, Condition Numbers, and Pseudospectra for the Multiparameter Eigenvalue Problem" in Linear Algebra and its Applications] extended perturbation theory from one-parameter eigenvalue problems to multiple spectral parameters. After two decades, we take it one step further and consider a different manifestation of the multiparameter eigenvalue problem that consists of one matrix equation with rectangular coefficient matrices. We perform a norm-wise backward error analysis, define condition numbers for both eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and introduce the pseudospectrum while also considering the computational implications of working with multiple spectral parameters. The rectangular shape hampers a direct application of the existing definitions and properties. For example, the left null space at a given eigenvalue is non-trivial and the dimensions of the left and right eigenvectors are different. Through numerical examples, we illustrate and link the different concepts from the perturbation theory. A system identification application seem to suggest that, in optimization-driven problems for which multiparameter reformulations exist, the globally optimal solutions tend to coincide with the best-conditioned eigenvalues.

AI.tldr: This paper extends multiparameter eigenvalue perturbation theory to rectangular matrix equations, developing backward error analysis, condition numbers, pseudospectra, and connecting well-conditioned eigenvalues to globally optimal solutions in optimization problems.
AI.motivation: To extend perturbation theory from standard multiparameter eigenvalue problems (square matrices) to rectangular multiparameter eigenvalue problems, which cannot be directly addressed by existing frameworks due to non-trivial left null spaces and differing left/right eigenvector dimensions.
AI.method: The authors perform norm-wise backward error analysis, define condition numbers for eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and introduce pseudospectra tailored to rectangular coefficient matrices. Numerical examples and a system identification application validate the theory.
AI.result: The paper establishes a coherent perturbation theory for rectangular multiparameter eigenvalue problems, showing that globally optimal solutions in optimization-driven problems tend to coincide with the best-conditioned eigenvalues.
AI.conclusion: Rectangular multiparameter eigenvalue problems require distinct perturbation analysis due to structural differences from the square case, and the condition number of eigenvalues can serve as a predictor for global optimality in certain optimization reformulations.

title: Addition Theorems for Real Vector Spherical Harmonics and Explicit Matrix Representations of the Quasi-Periodic Elastic Single Layer Potential
summary: This paper develops a multipole expansion method for the quasi-periodic elastic single layer potential $\mathcal{S}_D^{α,0}$ associated with the Kelvin tensor in one-dimensional periodic arrays. A key step in this approach is the derivation of translation addition theorems for the real vector spherical harmonics $V_{lm}$, $W_{lm}$, and $X_{lm}$. These addition theorems enable the exact calculation of all matrix entries of $\mathcal{S}_D^{α,0}$ in closed form. By working entirely within the spherical harmonic basis, the proposed analytical method overcomes the poor convergence and mesh-dependent issues commonly caused by the direct surface discretization of weakly singular kernels. Additionally, the involved infinite sums are evaluated exactly using polylogarithm functions, which eliminates the need for series truncation. As an application, the integral equation $\mathcal{S}_D^{α,0}[f]=\varphi$ is reduced to a linear system. This framework is further extended to dimer geometries consisting of two disjoint balls in each cell, where the off-diagonal matrices are explicitly formulated via the Lerch transcendent.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an analytical multipole expansion method for quasi-periodic elastic single layer potentials using vector spherical harmonics, translation addition theorems, and polylogarithm functions, eliminating truncation and mesh issues.
AI.motivation: To overcome poor convergence and mesh-dependent problems caused by direct surface discretization of weakly singular kernels in quasi-periodic elastic scattering problems.
AI.method: Derives translation addition theorems for real vector spherical harmonics (Vlm, Wlm, Xlm); uses polylogarithm functions for exact infinite sum evaluation; extends to dimer geometries via Lerch transcendent.
AI.result: All matrix entries of the quasi-periodic single layer potential are computed in closed form; the integral equation reduces to a linear system; off-diagonal dimer matrices are explicitly formulated.
AI.conclusion: The proposed analytical framework provides exact, convergent, and mesh-independent computation for quasi-periodic elastic problems, with applicability to complex geometries like dimer structures.

title: Smart strategies to navigate turbulent odor plumes reorienting to local wind
summary: Olfactory search in turbulent environments is a sensorimotor challenge solved with remarkable efficiency by many animals, yet replicating this ability in artificial systems remains difficult because detections are intermittent and wind direction fluctuates strongly, rendering standard search strategies unreliable. We introduce a wind-relative reinforcement-learning framework in which an agent navigates a turbulent plume with a single internal variable -- the elapsed time since the last odor detection -- and selects actions relative to a locally estimated wind direction filtered through an exponential memory kernel. Policies are trained and evaluated in direct numerical simulations of turbulence, capturing the multi-scale characteristics of velocity and odor fields in natural environments, both in the presence and absence of a mean wind. In a mild mean wind, the learned policy outperforms cast-and-surge regardless of the wind memory time, yet adapts its movement pattern to wind-estimation quality. In isotropic turbulence, performance peaks at an intermediate wind memory time, identifying temporal wind integration as a regime-dependent resource. Our results highlight the importance of developing and validating olfactory-navigation strategies under realistic turbulent conditions, and offer a compact design principle for minimal robotic olfactory navigation and testable predictions for biological search behavior.

AI.tldr: A wind-relative reinforcement learning framework with a single internal variable (time since last odor detection) enables efficient olfactory search in turbulent environments, outperforming classical cast-and-surge strategies and revealing optimal wind memory integration depending on turbulence conditions.
AI.motivation: Animals excel at olfactory search in turbulent environments, but artificial systems struggle because odor detections are intermittent and wind direction fluctuates unpredictably, making standard search strategies unreliable.
AI.method: A reinforcement-learning framework where an agent uses elapsed time since last odor detection as its sole internal state, selects actions relative to local wind direction filtered through an exponential memory kernel, and is trained/evaluated in direct numerical simulations of turbulence with and without mean wind.
AI.result: In mild mean wind, the learned policy outperforms cast-and-surge regardless of wind memory time; in isotropic turbulence, performance peaks at an intermediate wind memory time, indicating wind integration is a regime-dependent resource.
AI.conclusion: Highlights the necessity of realistic turbulent conditions for developing olfactory navigation strategies, and offers a minimal design principle for robotic olfactory navigation and testable predictions for biological search behavior.

title: Stochastic Galerkin and Monte-Carlo methods for parabolic problems: Numerical performance of variational matrix-free approximations
summary: Stochastic Galerkin methods offer unexplored potential for the numerical simulation of parabolic problems with random variables, in particular if they are combined with variational discretizations of the space and time variables. Due to the high dimensionality, the solution of the arising algebraic systems do not become feasible without efficient solvers, preconditioners, and software architectures. A stochastic Galerkin discretization with an embedded slabwise finite element approximation of the space and time variables is proposed and analyzed numerically. For solving the linear systems, GMRES iterations are block-preconditioned by a geometric multigrid (GMG) technique using a local Vanka smoother for the space-time subsystems. Monte-Carlo methods are also used for solving random parabolic problems and studied here for the purpose of comparison. The Monte-Carlo approach is built on the space-time finite element formulation together with the GMRES-GMG solver technology. All algorithms have been implemented in a unified matrix-free framework based on the deal.II software library. Comparative numerical evaluations illustrate the performance properties of both approaches, including convergence of the discretizations and statistics of the algebraic solver. Superiority of the stochastic Galerkin approach is observed.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes and evaluates a stochastic Galerkin method combined with space-time finite element discretization for solving parabolic problems with random variables, using GMRES with geometric multigrid preconditioning and Vanka smoothers, implemented in deal.II. Comparative results show superiority over Monte-Carlo methods.
AI.motivation: Stochastic Galerkin methods offer unexplored potential for numerical simulation of parabolic problems with random variables, but high dimensionality makes solving algebraic systems infeasible without efficient solvers, preconditioners, and software architectures.
AI.method: A stochastic Galerkin discretization with embedded slabwise finite element approximation in space and time, solved using GMRES iterations block-preconditioned by geometric multigrid (GMG) with local Vanka smoother for space-time subsystems. Monte-Carlo methods based on space-time FEM with GMRES-GMG solver are used for comparison, all implemented in a matrix-free framework in deal.II.
AI.result: Comparative numerical evaluations illustrate convergence properties of discretizations and statistics of the algebraic solver. Superiority of the stochastic Galerkin approach over Monte-Carlo methods is observed.
AI.conclusion: The stochastic Galerkin method combined with space-time finite element discretization and efficient GMG-GMRES solvers outperforms Monte-Carlo approaches for random parabolic problems.

title: Global well-posedness for 3D compressible and incompressible micropolar fluids without angular viscosity in strip domains
summary: This paper investigates an initial-boundary value problem for three-dimensional (3D) micropolar fluids in a strip domain, including both the compressible and the (homogeneous and inhomogeneous) incompressible cases in the absence of angular viscosity. The analysis is rendered difficult by two major obstacles: the degeneracy induced by vanishing angular viscosity, and the strong coupling between micro-rotation and velocity fields characterized by a non-dissipative anti-symmetric structure. Moreover, the presence of physical boundaries in the strip domain further compounds these obstacles. While the global well-posedness of the 2D incompressible Cauchy problem has been established in the literature, no results are available for the 3D system and the initial-boundary value problem in both two and three dimensions, particularly in the compressible case. By exploiting the intrinsic structure of the system and establishing delicate energy estimates, we overcome these difficulties and prove the global well-posedness of strong solutions near equilibrium in a strip domain.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global well-posedness of strong solutions for 3D micropolar fluids in a strip domain, covering compressible and incompressible cases without angular viscosity, by using energy estimates to overcome degeneracy and strong coupling challenges.
AI.motivation: To address the open problem of global well-posedness for 3D micropolar fluid systems with vanishing angular viscosity in a strip domain, for which no previous results existed especially for compressible and initial-boundary value problems.
AI.method: Exploiting the intrinsic structure of the system and establishing delicate energy estimates to handle degeneracy from vanishing angular viscosity, strong coupling through non-dissipative anti-symmetric structure, and physical boundary effects in the strip domain.
AI.result: Global well-posedness of strong solutions near equilibrium in a strip domain for 3D micropolar fluids (both compressible and incompressible cases) without angular viscosity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed energy-based approach successfully overcomes major obstacles (degeneracy, coupling, boundary effects) that previously prevented results for 3D systems and initial-boundary value problems, bridging a gap in the literature beyond the known 2D Cauchy problem.

title: RSE of a Quantum Transport Code and its Effects
summary: This paper presents our research software engineering (RSE) experiences over two years with libNEGF, a quantum transport code. We describe practical approaches to code quality assurance--including continuous integration, automated testing, and compiler warning correction--and performance engineering through continuous benchmarking. Our systematic application of these practices revealed critical defects: uninitialized memory reads, out-of-bounds writes, and notably, a misunderstood mathematical model in our boundary condition handling. We also document how continuous benchmarking exposed performance regressions caused by HPC system configuration changes. Our findings provide data points suggesting that a dangerous class of defects--equivalent to undefined behavior in C/C++ and processor-dependent behavior in Fortran--is as prevalent in Fortran scientific codes as elsewhere. While libNEGF is implemented in Fortran, most recommendations are applicable to scientific software regardless of implementation language, and they can be implemented selectively or in their entirety for both new and existing projects.

AI.tldr: This paper shares two years of research software engineering experience applying code quality assurance and performance engineering practices to libNEGF, a quantum transport code in Fortran. It reveals critical defects including uninitialized memory, out-of-bounds writes, and a misunderstood mathematical model, alongside performance regressions from HPC system changes.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate the importance and effectiveness of systematic software engineering practices (CI, automated testing, continuous benchmarking) in scientific computing, and to highlight that dangerous undefined/processor-dependent behaviors are as prevalent in Fortran scientific codes as in other languages.
AI.method: Applied code quality assurance (continuous integration, automated testing, compiler warning correction) and performance engineering (continuous benchmarking) systematically over two years to libNEGF, a Fortran-based quantum transport code.
AI.result: Discovered critical defects: uninitialized memory reads, out-of-bounds writes, and a misunderstood mathematical model in boundary condition handling. Also identified performance regressions caused by HPC system configuration changes. Found that undefined/processor-dependent behavior is common in Fortran scientific codes.
AI.conclusion: Systematic RSE practices are effective for scientific software quality and performance. Recommendations are language-agnostic and can be adopted selectively for new or existing projects.

title: A Bernoulli phase-fitted finite difference method and wavenumber-explicit analysis for the one-dimensional Helmholtz equation
summary: We propose a Bernoulli phase-fitted (BPF) finite difference method for the Helmholtz equation on the interval $(0, L)$ with impedance boundary conditions. The scheme is derived from a complexified Scharfetter--Gummel discretization of the one-way factorization of the Helmholtz operator. It yields both a phase-fitted interior discretization and exact discrete impedance boundary closures. For the homogeneous problem, the method is exact for plane waves, so the scheme introduces neither numerical dispersion in the interior nor artificial reflection at the boundaries. For the inhomogeneous problem, we prove well-posedness, derive wavenumber-explicit stability estimates, and establish second-order consistency and convergence valid for all $kh\notinπ\mathbb Z$, where $k$ is the wavenumber and $h$ the grid size. In particular, under the fixed-resolution condition $kh\le s_0$ for some $0<s_0<π$ together with $kL\geπ$, the constants in the error bounds remain uniform with respect to the wavenumber, yielding a pollution-free convergence theory in the principal Nyquist regime. Numerical experiments confirm the theoretical analysis and show favorable performance compared with standard and dispersion-corrected finite difference methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Bernoulli phase-fitted (BPF) finite difference method for the Helmholtz equation that is exact for plane waves in homogeneous problems (no numerical dispersion or artificial reflection) and provides wavenumber-explicit stability and pollution-free convergence in the Nyquist regime for inhomogeneous problems.
AI.motivation: To develop a finite difference method for the Helmholtz equation that eliminates numerical dispersion and artificial boundary reflections, and achieves wavenumber-uniform (pollution-free) convergence under practical resolution conditions.
AI.method: The method derives from a complexified Scharfetter-Gummel discretization of the one-way factorization of the Helmholtz operator, yielding phase-fitted interior discretization and exact discrete impedance boundary conditions.
AI.result: Proven well-posedness, wavenumber-explicit stability, and second-order convergence for all $kh\notin\pi\mathbb Z$. Under $kh\le s_0<\pi$ and $kL\ge\pi$, error bounds are uniform in wavenumber (pollution-free). Numerical experiments confirm superiority over standard and dispersion-corrected methods.
AI.conclusion: The BPF method is exact for homogeneous problems and pollution-free for inhomogeneous problems in the principal Nyquist regime, offering a robust alternative to existing finite difference schemes for the Helmholtz equation.

title: Peakon solutions and analytical properties for the Camassa-Holm type equations with quadratic nonlinearities
summary: In this paper, we derive the multi-peakon dynamical system of a class of Camassa-Holm-type equations with quadratic nonlinearities. We also consider the analytical properties for the Cauchy problem. Firstly, we establish local well-posedness of solutions in Besov spaces and then provide the blow-up criteria. Subsequently, we impose appropriate sufficient conditions on the initial data to guaranty that the corresponding solution either exists globally or blows up in a finite time. Finally, we prove the ill-posedness in the Besov space $B_{2,\infty}^{3/2}$ by utilizing the non-traveling wave solutions.

AI.tldr: This paper derives multi-peakon dynamics for a class of Camassa-Holm-type equations with quadratic nonlinearities, establishes local well-posedness and blow-up criteria in Besov spaces, provides conditions for global vs. finite-time blow-up, and proves ill-posedness in $B_{2,\infty}^{3/2}$ using non-traveling wave solutions.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamical behavior and analytical properties (well-posedness, blow-up, ill-posedness) of a class of Camassa-Holm-type equations with quadratic nonlinearities, particularly in Besov spaces.
AI.method: Derivation of multi-peakon dynamical systems, analysis in Besov spaces for local well-posedness and blow-up criteria, and use of non-traveling wave solutions to prove ill-posedness.
AI.result: Established local well-posedness and blow-up criteria in Besov spaces; identified sufficient conditions for global existence vs. finite-time blow-up; proved ill-posedness in $B_{2,\infty}^{3/2}$.
AI.conclusion: The analytical framework for this class of equations is advanced, with clear characterizations of solution behavior under different initial conditions and a sharp ill-posedness result in a critical Besov space.

title: Data-Efficient Neural Operator Training via Physics-Based Active Learning
comment: Presented at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Partial Differential Equations
summary: Solving partial differential equations with neural operators significantly reduces computational costs but remains bottlenecked by high training data requirements. Active learning offers a natural framework to mitigate this by selectively acquiring the most informative samples in an iterative manner. We introduce physics-based acquisition - a novel physics-informed active learning algorithm that leverages the partial differential equation residual to guide data selection. We validate the method by presenting numerical experiments for the 1D Burgers equation and the 2D compressible Navier-Stokes equations. We show that, in our experiments, physics-based acquisition consistently outperforms random acquisition and matches the state of the art in data efficiency. At the same time, it has the unique advantage of injecting a physics inductive bias into the training process, ensuring that simulation cost is spent where the model's physical understanding is weakest.

AI.tldr: Introduces physics-based acquisition, a physics-informed active learning algorithm using PDE residuals to guide data selection for neural operators, achieving state-of-the-art data efficiency on 1D Burgers and 2D Navier-Stokes equations.
AI.motivation: Neural operators reduce computational cost for solving PDEs but require large amounts of training data, creating a bottleneck that active learning can address.
AI.method: Physics-based acquisition leverages PDE residuals to selectively acquire the most informative training samples in an iterative active learning loop.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms random acquisition and matches state-of-the-art data efficiency on 1D Burgers and 2D compressible Navier-Stokes equations.
AI.conclusion: Physics-based acquisition injects a physics inductive bias into training, ensuring simulation cost targets areas where the model's physical understanding is weakest.

title: A Least-Squares Weak Galerkin Finite Element Scheme for Cauchy Problems in Helmholtz
comment: 20 pages, 9 tables, 3 figures. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2605.14770
summary: This paper introduces and rigorously analyzes a least-squares weak Galerkin (LS-WG) finite element method for the severely ill-posed Cauchy problem associated with the Helmholtz equation. By utilizing a weak Laplacian operator defined on a space of discontinuous functions, the proposed framework facilitates the seamless treatment of complex boundary conditions and internal interfaces. We emphasize the geometric flexibility of the LS-WG scheme on general polygonal and polyhedral partitions. Furthermore, we prove the uniqueness of the numerical solution and derive optimal-order error estimates with respect to a specifically designed discrete energy norm. Extensive numerical experiments validate the theoretical convergence rates and demonstrate the algorithm's robustness and efficiency over traditional Galerkin approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a least-squares weak Galerkin (LS-WG) method for solving the Cauchy problem of the Helmholtz equation. It uses weak Laplacian operators on discontinuous function spaces, handles complex boundaries and polygonal/polyhedral meshes, proves uniqueness and optimal error estimates, and validates through numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To address the severely ill-posed Cauchy problem for the Helmholtz equation, which traditional Galerkin methods struggle with due to instability and geometric complexity.
AI.method: A least-squares weak Galerkin (LS-WG) finite element method employing a weak Laplacian operator defined on discontinuous function spaces, enabling flexible polygonal/polyhedral partitions.
AI.result: Uniqueness of the numerical solution is proved; optimal-order error estimates under a discrete energy norm are derived; numerical experiments confirm theoretical convergence rates and show robustness/efficiency over traditional Galerkin methods.
AI.conclusion: The LS-WG method is a robust, geometrically flexible, and provably convergent approach for solving ill-posed Helmholtz Cauchy problems.

title: Local well-posedness for the two-and-a-half-dimensional EMHD system with split fractional dissipation
summary: We study the $2\frac12$-dimensional electron magnetohydrodynamics (EMHD) system on $\mathbb T^2$ with componentwise fractional dissipation: $\partial_t a+a_yb_x-a_xb_y=-Λ^αa$ and $\partial_t b-a_yΔa_x+a_xΔa_y=-Λ^βb$, where $0<α,β<2$. This system is a $2\frac12$-dimensional reduction of the magnetic equation in Hall--MHD/EMHD under the ansatz $B=\nabla\times(ae_z)+be_z$. We prove local well-posedness for initial data $(a_0,b_0)\in H^{s+1}(\mathbb T^2)\times H^s(\mathbb T^2)$ with $s\geq 2-\varepsilon$, provided that $α+β>2$. Thus neither component is required to carry a full Laplacian dissipation; the smoothing effects of the two fractional dissipations can be combined to control the Hall nonlinearity. The proof is based on Littlewood--Paley energy estimates, commutator bounds, and cancellations between the leading low--high interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a 2.5D EMHD system with fractional dissipation and proves local well-posedness when α+β>2, using Littlewood-Paley estimates and commutator bounds.
AI.motivation: To understand whether the combined smoothing effects of two fractional dissipations (α and β) can control the Hall nonlinearity, even when neither component has full Laplacian dissipation.
AI.method: The proof employs Littlewood-Paley energy estimates, commutator bounds, and cancellations between leading low-high interactions to establish local well-posedness.
AI.result: Local well-posedness holds for initial data (a0,b0) in H^{s+1}×H^s with s≥2−ε, provided that α+β>2.
AI.conclusion: The combined fractional dissipation (α+β>2) is sufficient to control the Hall nonlinearity, showing that neither component alone needs full Laplacian dissipation.

title: Achieving Material Robustness via Symmetric Stress Finite Element Discretizations
summary: When discretizing symmetric stress tensors in variational problems arising in continuum mechanics, one has to choose how to enforce the symmetry of the stress tensor: (i) strongly by requiring the discrete tensors to be pointwise symmetric or (ii) weakly by introducing a Lagrange multiplier. For $H(\mathrm{div})$-conforming finite element discretizations of Hellinger--Reissner elasticity and velocity--stress formulations of incompressible flow, where symmetry of the Cauchy stress tensor is tied to the conservation of angular momentum, we show that this choice may substantially impact the accuracy of the numerical scheme. Through a series of benchmark problems featuring anisotropic constitutive laws inspired by fiber reinforced material, liquid crystal polymer networks, and polar fluids, we show that schemes enforcing symmetry weakly can yield arbitrarily poor stress approximations -- even for zero-stress configurations. However, schemes enforcing symmetry strongly deliver accurate stress approximations independently of the constitutive law, a property we term material robustness. We present a unifying theory that rigorously explains this behavior.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that how stress tensor symmetry is enforced (strongly vs. weakly) in H(div)-conforming finite element methods significantly impacts accuracy, with weak enforcement leading to poor stress approximations for anisotropic materials, while strong enforcement ensures material-robust results.
AI.motivation: To investigate how the choice of enforcing symmetry of the stress tensor (strongly via pointwise symmetry vs. weakly via Lagrange multiplier) impacts numerical accuracy in H(div)-conforming discretizations for elasticity and incompressible flow problems, particularly for anisotropic materials.
AI.method: The authors compare strongly vs. weakly enforced symmetry in H(div)-conforming finite element discretizations for Hellinger-Reissner elasticity and velocity-stress formulations of incompressible flow. They test on benchmark problems with anisotropic constitutive laws (fiber-reinforced materials, liquid crystal polymer networks, polar fluids) and provide a unifying theory to explain the observed behavior.
AI.result: Weak enforcement of symmetry can yield arbitrarily poor stress approximations, even for zero-stress configurations, while strong enforcement delivers accurate approximations independent of the constitutive law (material robustness).
AI.conclusion: The choice of symmetry enforcement in H(div)-conforming FEM is critical; strong enforcement is materially robust, whereas weak enforcement can fail dramatically for anisotropic materials.

title: Instability estimates for the recovery of absorption in the diffusive regime of radiative transfer
summary: We revisit the instability properties of the recovery of the absorption coefficient for the radiative transfer equation in the diffusive regime. To this end, we develop a rather robust framework building on [Koch-Rüland-Salo, 2021] which allows us to deal with nonlinear critical stability transition phenomena. In particular, this permits us to consider rather general geometries based on the identification of compression properties of the forward operator. Given the albedo operator as the measurement data, we show that in the regime of vanishing Knudsen number there is a transition from Hölder to logarithmic stability in the inverse problem for the radiative transfer equation. As a central ingredient, we rely on suitable a priori estimates for the radiative transfer equation which we deduce by building on the strategy from [Demattè-Velázquez, 2025].

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes stability transitions (Hölder to logarithmic) in recovering the absorption coefficient for the radiative transfer equation in the diffusive regime, using compression properties and a priori estimates.
AI.motivation: To understand and characterize the instability properties of absorption coefficient recovery in the radiative transfer equation, especially the critical stability transition phenomena in the diffusive (small Knudsen number) regime.
AI.method: The authors build on the framework of Koch-Rüland-Salo (2021) and Demattè-Velázquez (2025), using compression properties of the forward operator and deriving a priori estimates to analyze stability transitions in general geometries.
AI.result: The paper shows that with albedo operator as measurement data, as the Knudsen number vanishes, there is a transition from Hölder stability to logarithmic stability in the inverse problem.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a robust framework for understanding nonlinear critical stability transitions in radiative transfer inverse problems, demonstrating a clear stability degradation in the diffusive regime.

title: Error analysis of a finite element scheme for parametric mean curvature flow based on the DeTurck trick
summary: The paper is concerned with the error analysis of a numerical scheme for the approximation of parametric mean curvature flow. The scheme we study is based on a reparametrization using the DeTurck trick and was proposed by Elliott and Fritz in [15]. In the semidiscrete case, for a spatial discretization by finite elements of order $k \geq 2$ we prove an optimal $H^1$-error estimate for the position vector. We present numerical experiments that confirm this error bound and demonstrate that the scheme has good properties with respect to the distribution of mesh points as already observed in [15].

AI.tldr: This paper proves optimal H1-error estimates for a DeTurck-based numerical scheme approximating parametric mean curvature flow, using finite elements of order k≥2, with numerical experiments confirming the error bounds and good mesh distribution properties.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous error analysis for the DeTurck-based numerical scheme proposed by Elliott and Fritz, which had been shown empirically to have good mesh quality but lacked theoretical convergence guarantees.
AI.method: Semidiscrete spatial discretization using finite elements of order k≥2, based on a reparametrization via the DeTurck trick to handle the parametric nature of mean curvature flow.
AI.result: Proved optimal H1-error estimates for the position vector; numerical experiments confirmed the theoretical error bounds and demonstrated good mesh point distribution properties.
AI.conclusion: The DeTurck-based finite element scheme for parametric mean curvature flow achieves optimal convergence rates in H1-norm and maintains favorable mesh quality, validating its theoretical and practical effectiveness.

title: Data-driven stress problem under purely normal homogeneous Neumann boundary conditions
summary: Data-Driven Continuum Mechanics -- the continuous counterpart of Data-Driven Computational Mechanics -- is a modern paradigm that enhances classical continuum mechanics by incorporating finite sets of experimental material data directly, avoiding any form of constitutive modeling. Despite recent progress, its analytical foundations remain at an early stage. In this work, we establish a rigorous functional-analytic framework for the data-driven stress problem under purely homogeneous normal Neumann boundary conditions. The problem is formulated as finding a stress field (satisfying the balance of linear and angular momenta and the boundary conditions) that is closest, in an $L^p$-sense, to an auxiliary stress field that is simultaneously sought and locally resembles a finite discrete set of experimental stress states. Our analysis relies on two key ingredients. First, the divergence operator induces a topological isomorphism between the space of symmetric stress fields modulo its kernel and the space of loads balanced by rigid-body motions, ensuring the existence of an equilibrated response. Second, the finiteness of the material data set guarantees proximinality in the stress space, which in turn yields a complete existence and uniqueness theory for solution equivalence classes. Together, these two properties provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for the data-driven stress problem under purely homogeneous normal Neumann boundary conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a rigorous functional-analytic framework for the data-driven stress problem under homogeneous normal Neumann boundary conditions, proving existence and uniqueness via divergence operator isomorphism and finiteness of material data.
AI.motivation: To provide rigorous mathematical foundations for Data-Driven Continuum Mechanics, which currently lacks analytical depth despite recent progress.
AI.method: Uses two key ingredients: (1) divergence operator induces a topological isomorphism between symmetric stress fields (mod kernel) and loads balanced by rigid-body motions; (2) finite material data sets guarantee proximinality in stress space, enabling existence and uniqueness theory for solution equivalence classes.
AI.result: A complete existence and uniqueness theory for solution equivalence classes in the data-driven stress problem under purely homogeneous normal Neumann boundary conditions.
AI.conclusion: The two properties (divergence isomorphism and finite-data proximinality) together provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for the data-driven stress problem in this specific setting.

title: Composite B-Spline Current Deposition and Interpolation Operators for Thin-Wire Finite-Difference Time-Domain Simulations
summary: Holland-Simpson thin-wire finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations of obliquely oriented closed-loop antennas exhibit persistent low-frequency parasitic currents because the current-deposition operator fails to conserve charge. Together with an interpolation operator that samples the tangential electric field along the wire, this deposition operator can be realized as a regularization of distributions against a regularized delta function supported on the wire. We show that charge conservation requires the deposited current to be discretely divergence-free when the wire carries a constant current, and we introduce a family of composite B-spline regularizations that satisfy this condition to machine precision. Exact evaluation of the coupling line integrals is achievable because the B-spline kernels are piecewise polynomial with breakpoints known a priori, allowing composite Gauss-Legendre quadrature with subinterval breakpoints at every grid-plane crossing. Taking the interpolation operator as the discrete adjoint of the deposition operator preserves skew-symmetry and ensures that a discretely irrotational electric field drives no net electromotive force around a closed loop. Numerical experiments on a center-fed dipole and on circular and square loop antennas show that the proposed regularizations yield orientation-independent impedance values consistent with known characteristics, whereas a naive trilinear regularization produces unphysical parasitic low-frequency currents in closed loops.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses parasitic low-frequency currents in FDTD simulations of obliquely oriented closed-loop antennas caused by charge non-conservation in the current-deposition operator, and proposes composite B-spline regularizations that ensure discrete charge conservation, skew-symmetry, and orientation-independent impedance results.
AI.motivation: Holland-Simpson thin-wire FDTD simulations of obliquely oriented closed-loop antennas exhibit persistent low-frequency parasitic currents because the current-deposition operator fails to conserve charge.
AI.method: The authors introduce a family of composite B-spline regularizations that enforce discrete charge conservation (deposited current is discretely divergence-free for constant wire currents) and use the discrete adjoint of the deposition operator as the interpolation operator to preserve skew-symmetry. They employ composite Gauss-Legendre quadrature with subinterval breakpoints at grid-plane crossings for exact evaluation of coupling line integrals.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on a center-fed dipole and on circular and square loop antennas show that the proposed regularizations yield orientation-independent impedance values consistent with known characteristics, whereas a naive trilinear regularization produces unphysical parasitic low-frequency currents in closed loops.
AI.conclusion: Composite B-spline regularizations that ensure discrete charge conservation and skew-symmetry effectively eliminate parasitic currents in closed-loop antenna FDTD simulations, achieving orientation-independent and physically consistent impedance results.

title: On the kinetic $p$-Laplace equation with nonlocal diffusion
summary: We study two nonlocal versions of the kinetic $p$-Laplace equation: a Gagliardo-type model defined through differences and a Bessel-type model defined via Fourier multiplication. Using critical kinetic trajectories, we derive representation formulas adapted to the kinetic transport-diffusion geometry and establish homogeneous and scale-invariant kinetic Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequalities for nonlocal diffusion, which yield gain-of-integrability estimates for weak solutions to the kinetic $p$-Laplace equations with nonlocal diffusion.

AI.tldr: This paper studies two nonlocal versions of the kinetic p-Laplace equation, deriving representation formulas and establishing Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequalities that yield gain-of-integrability estimates for weak solutions.
AI.motivation: To analyze nonlocal diffusion models in kinetic theory by extending the p-Laplace equation to nonlocal settings (Gagliardo-type and Bessel-type), and to obtain compactness/regularity properties for weak solutions.
AI.method: The authors use critical kinetic trajectories to derive representation formulas adapted to the kinetic transport-diffusion geometry, and establish homogeneous and scale-invariant kinetic Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequalities for nonlocal diffusion.
AI.result: Derivation of representation formulas and establishment of kinetic Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequalities that imply gain-of-integrability estimates for weak solutions to the kinetic p-Laplace equations with nonlocal diffusion.
AI.conclusion: The nonlocal kinetic p-Laplace models admit structural inequalities leading to improved integrability of weak solutions, advancing the theory of nonlocal diffusion in kinetic frameworks.

title: Entropy-stable discretizations for the compressible Euler equations using simple adaptive averages
summary: Entropy stabilization of the compressible Euler system is achieved by adapting the averages that are applied to the density and internal energy variables. The approach achieves non-linear robustness despite the use of simplified symmetric means (e.g., arithmetic, geometric, or harmonic evaluations), including their related expansions for asymptotic entropy conservation. The proposed formulation works via centralized convective terms and can naturally adhere to additional structures of the flow equations such as kinetic-energy- and pressure-equilibrium-preservation.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an entropy stabilization method for the compressible Euler system by adapting averages applied to density and internal energy variables, achieving non-linear robustness with simplified symmetric means while preserving kinetic energy and pressure equilibrium.
AI.motivation: To achieve entropy stabilization of the compressible Euler system through a novel averaging approach that maintains non-linear robustness and structural properties.
AI.method: The method adapts averages (arithmetic, geometric, or harmonic) applied to density and internal energy variables, using centralized convective terms and expansions for asymptotic entropy conservation.
AI.result: The approach achieves non-linear robustness despite using simplified symmetric means, and naturally preserves additional structures like kinetic-energy and pressure equilibrium.
AI.conclusion: The proposed formulation successfully stabilizes entropy in the compressible Euler system while adhering to additional physical constraints of the flow equations.

title: Ground states of the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger equation with a point interaction in dimensions 2 and 3
summary:
This paper is concerned with ground states of the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger equation with a point interaction, \[ \mathrm{i} \partial_t ψ=
-Δ_αψ+ ψ|ψ|^{p - 2} \quad \text{in} \quad \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R}^N, \] where $- Δ_α$ denotes the Laplacian of point interaction centered at the origin with inverse s-wave scattering length $- 2 (N - 1) πα$ and we suppose that either (i) $N = 2$, $α\in \mathbb{R}$ and $p > 2$ or (ii) $N = 3$, $α< 0$ and $2 < p < 3$. At sufficiently small masses, (i) we prove that this equation admits ground states, (ii) we obtain some qualitative properties of ground states and (iii) we obtain some results relating ground states with critical points of the associated action functional.

AI.tldr: This paper studies ground states of the defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger equation with a point interaction at small masses, proving existence, qualitative properties, and connections to action functional critical points in 2D (any α, p>2) and 3D (α<0, 2<p<3).
AI.motivation: To understand the interplay between nonlinear defocusing effects and singular point interactions in quantum systems, particularly how ground states behave at small mass regimes.
AI.method: The authors analyze the defocusing NLS with a point-interaction Laplacian (Δ_α) using variational and functional analytic techniques for two spatial dimensions N=2 and N=3 under specific parameter constraints.
AI.result: (i) Existence of ground states at sufficiently small masses, (ii) qualitative properties of these ground states, (iii) connections between ground states and critical points of the action functional.
AI.conclusion: The defocusing NLS with point interaction admits ground states in the small-mass regime, with well-characterized qualitative properties and variational structure, extending ground state theory to singular potential settings.

title: Experimental detection of inclusions for the time-harmonic elastic wave equation
summary: We are concerned with the reconstruction of inclusions in elastic bodies based on measurements from a laboratory experiment. In doing so, we solve the inverse problem of the time-harmonic elastic wave equation, in contrast to the stationary wave equation and the corresponding lab experiment proposed earlier in Eberle and Moll (2021). The investigation of the harmonic problem leads to a better reconstruction compared to the stationary one. Since we deal with real measurement data, we have to take into account, that those measurements always include measurement errors, so that we have to handle noisy data. Thus, we consider the linearized monotonicity method for noisy data and introduce a modified version of this method. Based on this, we reconstruct the inclusions numerically.

AI.tldr: This paper enhances inclusion reconstruction in elastic bodies by using time-harmonic elastic wave equations and a modified linearized monotonicity method that handles real measurement noise, achieving better results than the stationary approach.
AI.motivation: To improve the reconstruction of inclusions in elastic bodies by shifting from stationary to time-harmonic wave equations, while addressing real-world measurement noise in lab experiments.
AI.method: Solves the inverse problem of the time-harmonic elastic wave equation; introduces a modified version of the linearized monotonicity method to handle noisy measurement data.
AI.result: The time-harmonic approach yields better reconstructions than the stationary approach, and the modified monotonicity method effectively handles noisy real measurement data.
AI.conclusion: Using time-harmonic elastic wave equations with a noise-robust monotonicity method improves inclusion reconstruction in elastic bodies under realistic lab conditions.

title: Well-posedness and asymptotic limits for a degenerate Keller-Segel system with volume filling
summary: A class of parabolic-parabolic Keller-Segel systems with degenerate diffusion and volume filling is studied in a bounded domain subject to no-flux boundary conditions. The equations are derived from a multiphase fluid model. The interplay between nonlinear diffusion and density saturation leads to a rich variety of behaviors across different parameter regimes. We establish the existence of global weak solutions, a weak-strong uniqueness result, the exponential convergence to the homogeneous steady state, pattern formation in one spatial dimension, as well as the parabolic-elliptic and vanishing diffusion limits. The analysis relies on a priori estimates derived from suitable entropy functionals. Pattern formation is demonstrated by reducing the system to a first-order equation and conducting a detailed analysis of the resulting nonlinearity. Numerical simulations from a one-dimensional finite-volume scheme illustrate the asymptotic regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes a class of Keller-Segel systems with degenerate diffusion and volume filling, proving global existence of weak solutions, weak-strong uniqueness, exponential convergence to steady states, and pattern formation, supported by numerical simulations.
AI.motivation: To understand the interplay between nonlinear diffusion and density saturation in Keller-Segel systems derived from multiphase fluid models, and to characterize the rich variety of behaviors across different parameter regimes.
AI.method: The analysis uses a priori estimates from entropy functionals, reduces the system to a first-order equation for pattern formation, and employs a one-dimensional finite-volume scheme for numerical simulations.
AI.result: Established global weak solutions, weak-strong uniqueness, exponential convergence to homogeneous steady state, pattern formation in 1D, and parabolic-elliptic/vanishing diffusion limits.
AI.conclusion: The entropy-based approach effectively handles the degenerate diffusion and volume filling, revealing distinct asymptotic regimes and pattern formation behaviors validated by numerics.

title: Solving Multivariate Polynomial Systems and Rectangular Multiparameter Eigenvalue Problems with MacaulayLab
comment: Since the manuscript is currently undergoing review, we anticipate several improvements in the upcoming revision. In particular, the comparison with other software packages will be expanded significantly
summary: We present the Matlab toolbox MacaulayLab, which implements numerical linear algebra algorithms for solving multivariate polynomial systems and rectangular multiparameter eigenvalue problems. Its structure and functionality are the result of several years of research and algorithmic development. We demonstrate how the software works and compare its performance with other software packages, such as PNLA, PHCpack, and MultiParEig. Some core features of MacaulayLab are the fact that it solves two key problems via one common approach, works independently of the chosen polynomial basis and monomial order, and is capable of dealing with positive-dimensional solution sets at infinity. The toolbox (including its future updates) and a large collection of test problems are freely available online.

AI.tldr: MacaulayLab is a Matlab toolbox that uses numerical linear algebra to solve polynomial systems and multiparameter eigenvalue problems, outperforming PNLA, PHCpack, and MultiParEig by using a unified approach, supporting any polynomial basis/monomial order, and handling positive-dimensional solution sets at infinity.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified, efficient numerical linear algebra framework for solving multivariate polynomial systems and rectangular multiparameter eigenvalue problems, addressing limitations in existing software like PNLA, PHCpack, and MultiParEig.
AI.method: Numerical linear algebra algorithms implemented in Matlab; solves both polynomial systems and multiparameter eigenvalue problems via a common approach; independent of polynomial basis and monomial order; handles positive-dimensional solution sets at infinity.
AI.result: Performance comparison shows MacaulayLab competes favorably with PNLA, PHCpack, and MultiParEig; the toolbox is freely available online with a collection of test problems.
AI.conclusion: MacaulayLab offers a versatile, unified solution for polynomial system solving and eigenvalue problems, with advantages in basis independence and handling infinite solution sets.

title: Similarity Solutions for the Flux limited Keller Segel System with Time Varying Chemical Decay Rate
summary: We investigate a one dimensional flux limited Keller Segel system (FLKS) in which the chemical decay rate is allowed to vary explicitly in time, a feature motivated by enzymatic regulation and environmental variability in chemotactic signalling. Treating the decay rate as an arbitrary function, we carry out a systematic Lie symmetry analysis of the resulting PDE system and employ equivalence transformations to perform a complete group classification, we identify the kernel symmetry algebra admitted for arbitrary decay functions and determine three distinguished cases that extend the symmetry algebra constant decay rates, inverse time (power law) decay, and exponential decay. For each case, we construct an optimal system of subalgebras and derive the corresponding similarity reductions. Finally, we find some explicit solutions for our FLKS model. Our results provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for understanding which temporal decay patterns admit similarity reductions, thereby enabling analytical progress on flux limited chemotaxis models with realistic time varying degradation mechanisms.

AI.tldr: The paper performs a Lie symmetry analysis on a flux-limited Keller-Segel system with time-varying chemical decay rates, identifying three distinguished cases (constant, power-law, exponential decay) that admit symmetry extensions, and derives explicit solutions via similarity reductions.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for understanding temporal decay patterns in chemotactic signalling, motivated by enzymatic regulation and environmental variability where the chemical decay rate varies with time.
AI.method: Systematic Lie symmetry analysis combined with equivalence transformations for group classification; construction of optimal systems of subalgebras and similarity reductions.
AI.result: Identified kernel symmetry algebra for arbitrary decay functions; three distinguished cases (constant, power-law inverse time, exponential) that extend the symmetry algebra; explicit solutions derived via similarity reductions.
AI.conclusion: The findings enable analytical progress on flux-limited chemotaxis models with realistic time-varying degradation mechanisms by identifying which temporal decay patterns admit similarity reductions.

title: Precise Asymptotics and Exact Formulas for Tensor Product Energies of Fibonacci Lattices
summary:
We consider the asymptotics of sums of the form $$ \frac1{F_n^σ} \sum_{m = 1}^{F_n-1} \frac{f(m/F_n)}{\left|{\sin(πm/F_n)}\right|^σ} \frac{f(F_{n-1}m/F_n)}{\left|{\sin(πF_{n-1}m/F_n)}\right|^σ} $$ where $(F_n)_{n \in \mathbb N} = (1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, \dots)$ are the Fibonacci numbers. Such sums appear, for example, in the context of discrepancy theory and numerical integration methods reformulated as energy minimization problems.
  We show that for parameters $σ> 1$ and a large class of functions $f$ the above sum behaves asymptotically like $$ C n + D + O\left((1-\varepsilon)^{n}\right) $$ for some constants $C$ and $D$. These constants can be given via infinite series connected to the Dedekind zeta function over the algebraic number field $\mathbb Q(\sqrt5)$.
  In special cases we even observe simple closed-form expressions for such sums as above, explicitly proving that $$ \sum_{m=1}^{F_n-1} \frac1{\sin(πm/F_n)^2} \frac1{\sin(πF_{n-1} m/F_n)^2} = \frac{4n}{75} F_{2n} - \frac{17}{225}F_n^2 - (-1)^n \frac2{15} - \frac19. $$

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the asymptotic behavior of certain sums involving Fibonacci numbers and trigonometric functions, showing they behave like C n + D + O((1-ε)^n) for parameters σ>1, with constants expressed via infinite series related to Dedekind zeta functions over Q(√5). A special closed-form expression is derived.
AI.motivation: Such sums appear in discrepancy theory and numerical integration methods reformulated as energy minimization problems.
AI.method: Asymptotic analysis of sums over Fibonacci-indexed terms with trigonometric singularities, using properties of Fibonacci numbers and connections to the Dedekind zeta function over the algebraic number field Q(√5).
AI.result: For σ>1 and a broad class of functions f, the sum behaves asymptotically as C n + D + O((1-ε)^n). The constants C and D are given via infinite series related to the Dedekind zeta function. A special case yields a closed-form expression.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes precise asymptotic formulas for a class of Fibonacci-indexed trigonometric sums, with connections to algebraic number theory and explicit closed-form results.

title: A biquaternionic reformulation of Maxwell's equations via Fourier analysis
summary: We analyze the parabolic Dirac operator $D \pm i\partial_t$ in a biquaternionic setting, characterizing its kernel via generalized div-curl systems and Riemann-Cauchy-type relations between the real and imaginary parts. Using Fourier transforms, we fully characterize the Fourier transform of a fundamental solution of $D \pm i\partial_t$ and construct a well-defined right inverse operator. As a key application, we derive explicit vectorial solutions to the time-dependent Maxwell system, extending prior biquaternionic approaches. These tools offer analytical efficiency for complex electromagnetic problems.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the parabolic Dirac operator in a biquaternionic framework, develops fundamental solutions and right inverses via Fourier methods, and applies them to derive explicit time-dependent Maxwell system solutions.
AI.motivation: To extend prior biquaternionic approaches and provide analytically efficient tools for solving complex time-dependent electromagnetic problems.
AI.method: Biquaternionic formulation of the parabolic Dirac operator, characterization of its kernel via div-curl and Riemann-Cauchy relations, Fourier transform analysis of fundamental solutions, and construction of right inverse operators.
AI.result: Explicit vectorial solutions to the time-dependent Maxwell system are derived as a key application.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tools offer analytical efficiency for complex electromagnetic problems.

title: Universal thin-shell limits for the viscous operator on Riemannian hypersurfaces
summary: We decompose the ambient Bochner Laplacian acting on tangential vector fields on a thin shell around an arbitrary smooth hypersurface $M^n \hookrightarrow \R^{n+1}$ into an intrinsic piece and a radial boundary-shear piece. The intrinsic piece is the deformation Laplacian $Δ_B^{(n)} + \Ric^{(n)}$ on every hypersurface, regardless of extrinsic geometry. The boundary-shear piece is determined entirely by the normal profile of the velocity field. We prove that stress-free (Navier slip) boundary conditions yield the deformation Laplacian universally, and that Hodge (zero tangential vorticity) boundary conditions yield the Hodge Laplacian universally. Both results hold on any smooth hypersurface, not only on surfaces of constant curvature. This extends the sphere-specific results of Temam-Ziane and Miura to the general case and explains the extension-dependence found by Chan, Czubak, and Yoneda on the ellipsoid as a physical boundary-condition dependence. We also derive a continuous one-parameter family of boundary conditions interpolating between the two limits, producing an effective viscous operator $Δ_α= Δ_\Def - 2α\,\Ric - 4α(1-α)S^2$ that couples to the extrinsic geometry (through the shape operator squared) only in the intermediate partial-slip regime.

AI.tldr: This paper decomposes the Bochner Laplacian on thin shells around hypersurfaces into intrinsic (deformation Laplacian) and boundary-shear parts, showing that stress-free boundary conditions yield the deformation Laplacian and Hodge conditions yield the Hodge Laplacian universally, with a one-parameter family interpolating between them.
AI.motivation: To extend prior sphere-specific results (Temam-Ziane, Miura) to arbitrary smooth hypersurfaces and explain the extension-dependence found by Chan, Czubak, and Yoneda on ellipsoids as a physical boundary-condition dependence.
AI.method: Decomposition of the ambient Bochner Laplacian acting on tangential vector fields on a thin shell around an arbitrary smooth hypersurface M^n embedded in R^{n+1} into intrinsic (deformation Laplacian + Ricci curvature) and radial boundary-shear components.
AI.result: Stress-free boundary conditions yield the deformation Laplacian universally; Hodge boundary conditions yield the Hodge Laplacian universally on any smooth hypersurface. A continuous one-parameter family of boundary conditions produces effective viscous operator Δ_α = Δ_Def - 2α Ric - 4α(1-α)S^2.
AI.conclusion: The results extend previous sphere-specific findings to general hypersurfaces and show that the extension-dependence is actually boundary-condition dependence, with extrinsic geometry coupling only in the intermediate partial-slip regime.

title: Atomic Characterization and Its Applications of Matrix-Weighted Variable Hardy Spaces
summary: In this article, by means of the matrix-weighted grand maximal function we first introduce the variable Hardy space $H^{p(\cdot)}_W$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the $\mathscr{A}_{p(\cdot),\infty}$ matrix weight $W$ and with the variable exponent $p(\cdot)$ having globally log-Hölder continuity, and then via using several different convex body valued maximal functions we establish its various maximal function equivalent characterizations. By combining a refined Whitney decomposition with both the convex body valued maximal function and its corresponding convex-body reducing operator, we obtain the atomic characterization of $H^{p(\cdot)}_W$. As applications, we obtain its dual space and establish the boundedness of Calderón--Zygmund operators from $H^{p(\cdot)}_W$ to $L^{p(\cdot)}_W$ and to itself. This approach to establish atomic characterization is different from all previous ones.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces variable Hardy spaces with matrix weights, establishes maximal function and atomic characterizations, and proves boundedness of Calderón-Zygmund operators on these spaces.
AI.motivation: To extend the theory of variable Hardy spaces to the setting of matrix weights, which is a non-trivial generalization requiring new techniques due to the non-commutative nature of matrix-valued functions.
AI.method: Uses matrix-weighted grand maximal functions, convex body valued maximal functions, and a refined Whitney decomposition combined with convex-body reducing operators to establish atomic characterizations.
AI.result: Obtained maximal function equivalent characterizations, atomic characterization, dual space description, and boundedness of Calderón-Zygmund operators on the variable Hardy space with matrix weights.
AI.conclusion: Successfully developed a novel approach (different from all previous ones) for atomic characterization in the matrix-weighted variable exponent setting, with applications to duality and operator boundedness.

title: On the Regularity and Generalization of One-Step Wasserstein-guided Generative Models for PDE-Induced Measures
summary: Despite the remarkable empirical success of generative models, the available theory on their statistical accuracy in scientific computing remains largely pessimistic. This paper develops a theoretical framework for understanding the regularity of transport maps and the generalization properties of one-step Wasserstein-guided generative models for PDE-induced probability measures. We consider normalized target densities associated with linear elliptic and parabolic equations on bounded domains, as well as diffusion and Fokker--Planck equations on the torus. Under standard structural assumptions, we prove that these target measures satisfy doubling conditions. By combining this fact with regularity theory for optimal transport between doubling measures, we show that the optimal transport map from a uniform source measure to the target measure is Hölder continuous. This regularity yields an approximation-theoretic justification for one-step generative models that learn PDE-induced distributions via a single pushforward map. As a representative instance, we study DeepParticle and derive excess-risk bounds characterizing the discrepancy between the learned map and the population-optimal map. We also establish a robustness estimate under target shift and illustrate the theory with experiments which support the derived rates.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a theoretical framework showing that transport maps for PDE-induced probability measures are Hölder continuous, providing statistical guarantees for one-step Wasserstein-guided generative models like DeepParticle.
AI.motivation: Existing theory on generative models' statistical accuracy in scientific computing is pessimistic; this work aims to bridge the gap between empirical success and theoretical understanding for PDE-induced distributions.
AI.method: The authors prove that target densities from linear elliptic/parabolic PDEs and diffusion/Fokker-Planck equations satisfy doubling conditions, then leverage optimal transport regularity theory to show Hölder continuity of the transport map from uniform to target measure, yielding excess-risk bounds for DeepParticle.
AI.result: Optimal transport maps for PDE-induced measures are Hölder continuous; excess-risk bounds are derived for DeepParticle; robustness under target shift is established; experiments support theoretical rates.
AI.conclusion: The theory provides an approximation-theoretic justification for one-step generative models learning PDE-induced distributions, offering a more optimistic theoretical perspective than previously available.

title: Uniform-in-time propagation of chaos for Second-Order Consensus-Based Optimization
summary: We study second-order Consensus-Based Optimization (CBO), a derivative-free global optimization algorithm in which both the consensus drift and the multiplicative exploratory noise act on the particle velocities. We establish the first quantitative uniform-in-time propagation of chaos results for the second-order CBO dynamics, together with an almost uniform-in-time stability estimate for the microscopic particle system. The main difficulty is that in the second-order model, both the consensus mechanism and the stochastic forcing act only on the velocity variable, while the position evolves via transport. As a result, no direct coercive mechanism is available on the spatial component, and, combined with the shift-invariant nature of the consensus interaction, a standard synchronous-coupling argument in the Euclidean phase-space distance cannot be closed uniformly in time. To overcome this difficulty, we develop a hypocoercive coupling framework together with a suitable shifting of variables. Passing to centered internal variables decouples the fluctuation dynamics from the empirical mean and yields exponential decay of centered moments, which is the key ingredient for uniform-in-time control. Combined with uniform-in-time raw moment bounds, concentration inequalities, stability estimates for the weighted mean, and a Monte Carlo estimate, we achieve the classical Monte Carlo rate for propagation of chaos uniformly in time. In addition, the system-to-system stability estimate avoids the mean-field sampling error and yields the faster convergence rate O(J^{-q}).

AI.tldr: This paper establishes the first quantitative uniform-in-time propagation of chaos results for second-order Consensus-Based Optimization (CBO), overcoming challenges from velocity-only consensus/stochastic forcing via a hypocoercive coupling framework and centered variable shifting.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of uniform-in-time propagation of chaos results for second-order CBO, where consensus and noise only affect velocities (not positions), making standard coupling arguments fail due to no direct coercive spatial mechanism and shift-invariant consensus.
AI.method: Developed a hypocoercive coupling framework with variable shifting to center internal variables, decoupling fluctuation dynamics from empirical mean, enabling exponential decay of centered moments, combined with uniform moment bounds, concentration inequalities, and stability estimates.
AI.result: Achieves classical Monte Carlo rate for propagation of chaos uniformly in time, and a faster O(J^{-q}) convergence rate for system-to-system stability that avoids mean-field sampling error.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hypocoercive approach successfully resolves the theoretical challenges unique to second-order CBO dynamics, providing rigorous uniform-in-time guarantees.

title: Sharp Gaussian Isoperimetry along a Ricci Flow
summary: We prove the sharp Gaussian isoperimetric inequality for conjugate heat-kernel measures along a Ricci flow via a monotonicity formula. As consequences, we obtain the exact Gaussian enlargement theorem and a Gaussian-quantile two-set concentration estimate. In particular, this recovers the exponential concentration estimate of Hein--Naber from a sharper isoperimetric profile. We also derive Gaussian rearrangement inequalities, recover the sharp Hein--Naber log-Sobolev inequality, and identify the universal Gaussian-model constants in Bamler's \(L^p\)-Poincaré inequalities. Further applications include Gaussian-profile localization near Bamler's \(H_n\)-centers, convex-order and moment estimates for logarithmic derivatives of the conjugate heat kernel, reverse hypercontractivity, entropy-regular profile stability, and a path-space Bobkov inequality.

AI.tldr: The paper proves a sharp Gaussian isoperimetric inequality for conjugate heat-kernel measures along Ricci flow via monotonicity, yielding numerous applications including concentration estimates, rearrangement inequalities, log-Sobolev and Poincaré inequalities, localization estimates, and entropy stability results.
AI.motivation: To establish sharp isoperimetric inequalities for heat-kernel measures along Ricci flow, extending classical Gaussian isoperimetry to the geometric flow setting and unifying various known estimates.
AI.method: Proves a monotonicity formula that yields the sharp Gaussian isoperimetric inequality for conjugate heat-kernel measures along a Ricci flow.
AI.result: Sharp Gaussian isoperimetric inequality, Gaussian enlargement theorem, two-set concentration estimate, exponential concentration (Hein-Naber), Gaussian rearrangement inequalities, sharp log-Sobolev inequality (Hein-Naber), universal constants in Bamler's L^p-Poincaré inequalities, localization near H_n-centers, convex-order/moment estimates, reverse hypercontractivity, entropy-regular profile stability, and path-space Bobkov inequality.
AI.conclusion: A single monotonicity formula along Ricci flow generates a wide array of sharp functional inequalities and geometric estimates, demonstrating the unifying power of isoperimetric thinking in geometric analysis.

title: Relative trace formulas for obstacle scattering with Neumann and transmission boundary conditions
summary: We consider the case of scattering by several obstacles in $\mathbb{R}^d$ for $d \geq 2$. We establish a relative trace formula for Neumann and transmission boundary conditions analogous to the one obtained in arXiv:2002.07291 for Dirichlet boundary conditions. In the case of $f(x) = x^{1/2}$ the trace has the interpretation of the Casimir energy of the obstacle configuration. In the one-dimensional case, we recover a rigorous version of the Lifshitz formula for the Casimir energy of parallel plates with frequency-independent electric permittivity and magnetic permeability. We thereby strengthen the mathematical foundations of the Casimir effect and demonstrate the flexibility of the rigorous approach established in arXiv:2104.09763 and arXiv:2002.07291.

AI.tldr: This paper extends a relative trace formula from Dirichlet to Neumann and transmission boundary conditions for scattering by multiple obstacles, with applications to Casimir energy calculations. In 1D, it rigorously recovers the Lifshitz formula for parallel plates.
AI.motivation: To strengthen the mathematical foundations of the Casimir effect and demonstrate the flexibility of prior rigorous approaches by extending them to new boundary conditions (Neumann and transmission).
AI.method: The authors establish a relative trace formula for Neumann and transmission boundary conditions, analogous to a previous result for Dirichlet conditions, using scattering theory in ℝ^d (d≥2).
AI.result: For f(x)=x^{1/2}, the trace corresponds to Casimir energy. In 1D, they rigorously recover the Lifshitz formula for Casimir energy of parallel plates with frequency-independent electric permittivity and magnetic permeability.
AI.conclusion: The approach strengthens mathematical foundations of the Casimir effect and shows the flexibility of the rigorous framework from earlier works.
